blindAnswer: A trapdoor (back door) is a hidden, undocumented entry point deliberately built into a program, OS or service that bypasses normal authentication/security controls.
reason: Standard security definition: covert undocumented access mechanism, synonymous with back door.
sourceAnswer: A computer trapdoor, also known as a back door, provides a secret -- or at least undocumented -- method of gaining access to an application, operating system or online service.

blindAnswer: Propagation delay = time for a bit to travel across the physical medium (distance / propagation speed). Rate = the link's data transfer speed (bits per second). Throughput = the actual amount of data successfully delivered per unit time.
reason: Textbook network-delay definitions; source's phrasing (last bit reaching destination; units in bytes/sec for rate) is loose but substantively the same.
sourceAnswer: Propagation delay: time taken by the last bit of the packet to reach the destination. Rate: speed at which data is transferred, measured in bytes per second. Throughput: measure of how many units of information a system can process in a given amount of time.

reason: Hand-drawn table is partly garbled (B column is four identical up-arrows/clock edges; Qnext reads Q, Q(or Q-bar), 0, 1). But it has TWO inputs, which rules out T (single input), and every row has a defined output with no forbidden/invalid state, which rules out SR; only JK has a fully-defined 4-row table covering hold/reset/set/toggle.

unanswerable - 10010 base 2 = 18 decimal (or -14 as 5-bit two's complement); neither offered option (-37, -27) can be produced from the stated binary value under any standard interpretation, and the stem itself is hedged ('i think it was').

802.3 is wired Ethernet, the only non-wireless option and the intended odd one out; note the question is technically flawed since 802.15 (WPAN/Bluetooth) and 802.16 (WiMAX) are also not Wi-Fi, so strictly three options satisfy 'not a WiFi standard'.

blindAnswer: A collision occurs when two distinct keys hash to the same index/slot in the hash table.
reason: Standard definition; source says 'two keys map to the same value', which is the same idea (loosely worded - it is the same slot/hash, not the same stored value).
sourceAnswer: When two keys map to the same value

reason: SMTP (send), POP3 and IMAP (retrieve) are all email protocols, so 'All the above'.
Compiled and ran it: 0 iterations. x<=1 && x>=10 is unsatisfiable for any integer, so the body never executes.

blindAnswer: Assuming 'ready' state: the thread has everything it needs to run except the CPU; it sits in the ready queue waiting for the scheduler to dispatch it.
reason: Stem is a meta-note ('Operating Systems questions about...') rather than an actual question, and 'read' is presumably a typo for 'ready'; the exact question asked is not recorded, so any answer is a guess at intent.

blindAnswer: A function (also called a method, procedure, subroutine, or routine).
reason: A named block of code performing one specific task is a function; the item's own subtopic is 'functions', supporting that reading.
SQL is the query/programming language of relational DBMSs; HTML, XML and CSS are markup/styling languages.
Solaris, Linux and Windows are operating systems; Oracle is a database vendor/RDBMS.
O(lg n) grows slowest of the four, so it is the best complexity.
Big-O drops constant factors and lower-order terms: O(2000n - 1) = O(n).
Tree, heap and list are data structures; bubble sort is an algorithm.
Inner loop runs i times for each i = 1..n, so total calls = 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2.
Two recursive calls on n-1 and n-2 plus O(1) work for the test and addition: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1.
Hit ratio = hits / total accesses = 30 / (30 + 40) = 0.4286.

blindAnswer: Recursion is a technique in which a function calls itself, solving a problem by reducing it to smaller instances of the same problem until a base case is reached.
reason: Standard definition; matches source.
sourceAnswer: A method where the solution to a problem depends on solutions to smaller instances of the same problem.

blindAnswer: Multitasking is the OS capability of executing multiple tasks/processes apparently at the same time by rapidly switching the CPU among them.
reason: Same concept as the source's user-facing phrasing ('perform more than one computer task at the same time').
sourceAnswer: Allowing a user to perform more than one computer task at the same time.
